articles.title: National council approves guidelines for AI in education and bans 'high-risk' use with students
articles.summary: The National Education Council (CNE) approved an initial opinion on May 11, 2026, establishing guidelines for the use of artificial intelligence in basic and higher education. The guidelines position AI as a support tool requiring human supervision and prohibit its “high-risk” use with students, though final approval by the Ministry of Education (MEC) remains pending after further discussions.
articles.insight:
This development signals growing regulatory caution around AI in education in key source markets, potentially influencing student perceptions of academic integrity and institutional support—though direct impacts on demand or visa rules remain uncertain without further policy implementation.  
Admissions teams should monitor how national AI guidelines evolve in priority countries, especially regarding assessments and academic support, and consider proactively clarifying their institutions’ responsible AI practices to address prospective students’ emerging concerns about fairness and oversight.

articles.title: 200-year-old UK institution to open Ho Chi Minh City campus
articles.summary: King's College School Wimbledon, a 200-year-old UK institution, has announced plans to open a new campus in Ho Chi Minh City. This expansion marks the school’s entry into Southeast Asia.
articles.insight:
This move signals growing institutional confidence in Vietnam’s education market and may reflect rising middle-class demand for premium Western-aligned schooling, though it doesn’t directly indicate shifting university preferences—student pathways from such schools to tertiary institutions remain uncertain and culturally mediated.  
Admissions teams should track enrolment patterns, parent surveys, and local media sentiment around this campus to gauge whether it amplifies aspirational demand for UK degrees—or conversely, accelerates domestic or regional higher education alternatives among its graduates.

articles.title: US to end “Duration of Status” for F, J, and I visas and limit the time international students can study in the US
articles.summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security proposed in August 2025 to eliminate “Duration of Status” for F, J, and I visas, replacing it with fixed time limits for international students. Under the proposal, most international students would be required to complete their programs by September 2026—typically within four years—unless granted an extension by U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services.
articles.insight:
This proposed U.S. policy shift—replacing open-ended “Duration of Status” with fixed, time-limited visas for F, J, and I holders—may dampen U.S. appeal for students from regions prioritising flexibility or longer study-to-work pathways, though actual enrolment impact depends on final rule details and global alternatives.  
Admissions teams should track the rule’s progression through federal review, assess how peer institutions in the UK, Australia, and New Zealand position their visa frameworks, and begin scenario-planning for potential shifts in application volumes from high-intent markets like India and China.

articles.title: Vietnamese student wins fully funded PhD at world's No. 8 university in Singapore
articles.summary: A 22-year-old Vietnamese chemistry student has secured a fully funded PhD fellowship at the National University of Singapore, ranked eighth globally. She completed her bachelor’s degree six months ahead of schedule before earning the fellowship.
articles.insight:
This news signals growing competitiveness in Southeast Asia’s high-achieving student segment, where accelerated academic pathways and elite Asian institutions may be reshaping destination preferences—though it remains unclear whether this reflects broader regional shifts or an isolated success story.  
Admissions teams should monitor application trends from Vietnam and neighbouring countries for changes in yield rates and fellowship uptake, while reviewing scholarship visibility and articulation pathways for accelerated undergraduates to ensure their offerings remain compelling amid rising regional alternatives.

articles.title: Australia accused of profiting millions of dollars from rising student visa rejections and application fees
articles.summary: Australia collected nearly AU$10 million (US$7.25 million) in non-refundable visa application fees from rejected student visa applications in a single month. This revenue has drawn criticism for appearing to profit from the educational aspirations of foreign students.
articles.insight:
This news may dampen Australia’s appeal among cost-conscious international students, particularly from Asia and Africa, as perceptions of visa rejection risks and non-refundable fees could shift demand toward perceived more predictable destinations—though direct causal links to enrolment trends remain unproven.  
Admissions teams should track real-time visa grant rates, student sentiment in key source markets via social listening, and competitor messaging around visa support—while avoiding premature policy changes until clearer evidence of behavioural shifts emerges.

articles.title: Chinese students rethink traditional study-abroad destinations
articles.summary: The number of Chinese students studying overseas last year declined by nearly 20% compared to its pre-pandemic peak. This drop is attributed to rising costs, stricter visa policies, and increased appeal of domestic education opportunities.
articles.insight:
The 20% decline in Chinese outbound students signals potential softening demand for traditional Anglophone destinations, though it remains unclear how much reflects temporary pandemic-related disruption versus longer-term shifts in cost sensitivity, visa perceptions, or domestic alternatives.  
Admissions teams should monitor quarterly application trends from China, track policy developments on visas and post-study work rights, and assess how domestic university quality improvements and scholarship offerings influence student decision-making—without assuming irreversible market contraction.
